topic_summary: Departing Australia Superannuation Payment (DASP) — a menu of distinct situations a temporary-resident leaver can land on, covering eligibility, tax rates by visa type, the unclaimed-super transfer trigger (6 months), paper-application ID thresholds ($5,000), processing timelines (28 days payment / 14 days payment summary), and what happens when super is held across multiple funds. Every dish is grounded in the ATO DASP authority (snapshot 23de057f-7b08-4fcd-9b3c-4f5c19fac179) and a verbatim market signal.

questions.text: Which of the following best describes your situation with Australian superannuation?

options.label: I held a temporary resident visa, my visa has ceased, I have left (or am about to leave) Australia, and I am not an Australian/New Zealand citizen or permanent resident
I am an Australian citizen, New Zealand citizen, or Australian permanent resident
I held visa subclass 405 or 410
I am not sure whether I meet all the eligibility conditions
None of these describe me

questions.text: What is your main question about your Departing Australia Superannuation Payment (DASP)?

options.label: How much tax will be deducted from my DASP?
My super hasn't been paid yet — I want to know when my fund sends it to the ATO
What ID documents do I need for a paper application?
How long will it take to receive my payment after I apply?
I have super in more than one fund — how does DASP work across all of them?
I am not sure / my question is different

questions.text: What type of temporary visa did you hold when your super contributions were made?

options.label: Working Holiday Maker visa (subclass 417 or 462) — for all or part of my time
A temporary visa other than subclass 417 or 462 (e.g. student, skilled, sponsored worker)
I am not sure which visa subclass I held

questions.text: Has it been 6 months or more since you left Australia AND your visa has ceased?

options.label: Yes — it has been 6 months or more since I left and my visa has ceased
No — it has been less than 6 months since I left, or my visa has not yet ceased
I am not sure how long it has been or whether my visa has ceased

questions.text: Is your super balance $5,000 or more?

options.label: Yes — my balance is $5,000 or more
No — my balance is below $5,000
I do not know my current balance

questions.text: Did you hold ONLY a Working Holiday Maker visa (subclass 417 or 462), or did you also hold other temporary visa types during your time in Australia?

options.label: I held only a Working Holiday Maker visa (subclass 417 or 462) the entire time
I held a Working Holiday Maker visa AND another temporary visa type at different times
I am not sure of my full visa history

terminals.heading: Am I eligible for DASP? — visa, departure, and citizenship gate
terminals.result_copy: Review recommended: your answers indicate uncertainty about one or more of the four DASP eligibility conditions (temporary visa type, visa cessation, departure from Australia, and citizenship/residency status). This dish outlines each condition — including the excluded visa subclasses 405 and 410 — so you can assess whether a DASP claim is indicated for your situation.

terminals.heading: How much tax is deducted? — standard (non-WHM) visa holders: 35% and 45%
terminals.result_copy: Indicated result: for temporary visa holders who are not Working Holiday Makers, the taxable taxed element of your DASP is subject to a 35% rate and the taxable untaxed element is subject to a 45% rate. Review recommended to confirm which elements apply to your specific super fund balance.

terminals.heading: How much tax is deducted? — Working Holiday Maker (subclass 417/462): 65% on both elements
terminals.result_copy: Indicated result: for Working Holiday Maker (subclass 417 or 462) visa holders, both the taxable taxed element and the taxable untaxed element are subject to a 65% rate. Where a DASP spans both WHM and non-WHM periods, each fund individually assesses which contributions were made during a WHM visa period. Review recommended to confirm how your fund will attribute contributions across visa periods.

terminals.heading: I haven't claimed yet — when does my fund send my super to the ATO?
terminals.result_copy: Indicated result: if 6 months or more have passed since you left Australia and your visa has ceased, your fund is required to transfer your super to the ATO as unclaimed super money — you would then claim from the ATO rather than your fund. If that threshold has not yet been reached, claiming directly from your fund may still be available. Review recommended to confirm the current status of your super and which pathway applies.

terminals.heading: My super balance is $5,000 or more — what extra ID documents do I need for a paper application?
terminals.result_copy: Indicated result: for paper applications where your super balance is $5,000 or more, your fund may require certified copies of proof-of-identification documents and may also require a Certification of Immigration Status from Home Affairs. Document requirements depend on the fund and the pathway. Review recommended — the authority notes it is easier to certify documents while still in Australia.

terminals.heading: How long will it take to receive my DASP payment and payment summary?
terminals.result_copy: Indicated result: a completed DASP application is generally paid within 28 days of receipt. The payer must then issue a DASP payment summary within 14 days of making the payment. Review recommended to confirm your application is complete, as the 28-day window begins only upon receipt of a completed application.

terminals.heading: I have super in multiple funds — how does DASP work across all of them?
terminals.result_copy: Indicated result: each super fund individually assesses your DASP application, including any WHM mixed-visa attribution. The DASP online system automatically confirms immigration status with the Department of Home Affairs for each fund. Review recommended to ensure a separate application is lodged for each fund holding your super.

terminals.heading: DASP may not apply to your situation
terminals.result_copy: Your answers suggest DASP may not fit — for example you are an Australian or New Zealand citizen or permanent resident, or you held an excluded visa subclass (405 or 410), so the ordinary Australian super rules apply instead. A short personalised review works through what does apply to your circumstances and the steps to take next.

terminals.heading: Your situation needs a closer look
terminals.result_copy: Your answers do not yet confirm which DASP position applies to you — a key detail (such as your visa type, how long since you left, or your balance) is unconfirmed. A short personalised review confirms where you stand and exactly what to check before you claim.
